      Nickel guy
   =20
 =20
If you sell nickel futures at a price of $25,000 per ton, and then the pric=
e of nickel futures goes up to $100,000 per ton, then in some simple arithm=
etic sense you have lost $75,000 per ton. If you sold 100 tons of nickel fu=
tures, then you have lost more than $7=C2=A0million. But if you sold 150,00=
0 tons=C2=A0of futures, the math changes a bit; it becomes non-linear and r=
elativistic. If you sold 150,000 tons of nickel futures at $25,000 per ton,=
 and then the price goes up to $100,000, your banks will call you up and sa=
y =E2=80=9Cuh you have lost $11 billion, can you pay that please,=E2=80=9D =
and you will say =E2=80=9CI would prefer not to,=E2=80=9D and an insane ser=
ies of events will happen:=09The nickel exchange will   cancel a bunch of t=
rades and declare that=C2=A0actually=C2=A0the market price of nickel is $48=
,000 per ton, magically reversing most=C2=A0of your losses.=09Then the exch=
ange will call you and say =E2=80=9Cokay let=E2=80=99s close you out of tha=
t trade at $48,000 per ton.=E2=80=9D=09Then you will say =E2=80=9Cno, this =
is still too much money for me to lose, I prefer not to.=E2=80=9D=09Then yo=
ur banks will say =E2=80=9Cwell okay how much are you willing to lose?=E2=
=80=9D=09You will say =E2=80=9CI would close out this trade at $30,000, tha=
t=E2=80=99s how much money I am willing to lose.=E2=80=9D=09Your banks will=
 say =E2=80=9Cokay fine, we=E2=80=99ll wait for nickel prices to go back be=
low $30,000, meanwhile we=E2=80=99ll just lend you the money to stay in the=
 position.=E2=80=9D=09They will.=09Eventually nickel prices will go below $=
30,000 and you will get out of the trade at a modest loss.=09If prices neve=
r go below $30,000 then I guess your banks are very sad, but honestly they=
=E2=80=99re pretty sad about all of this anyway.I cannot stress enough that=
 this is not how it works if you are a small customer. This is the white-gl=
ove treatment that only the biggest customers get. If you are big enough, y=
ou get to=C2=A0tell the exchange how much money you=E2=80=99re willing to l=
ose, and the exchange and your banks will make sure you don=E2=80=99t lose =
more than that.Here is a wild  Bloomberg News story about Xiang Guangda, th=
e Chinese metals tycoon who runs Tsingshan Holding Group Co., who is nickna=
med =E2=80=9CBig Shot,=E2=80=9D=C2=A0and who=C2=A0blew up the London Metals=
 Exchange in March.  We  talked  about it  at the  time, but this story add=
s a lot more detail about what Xiang, his bankers and the LME were thinking=
 and doing. It is not pretty!=C2=A0Xiang shorted something like 150,000 ton=
s of nickel somewhere in the $20,000s, and when nickel prices went up to $1=
00,000 he said =E2=80=9Cno thank you=E2=80=9D:After nickel started spiking =
on March 7, Tsingshan struggled to meet its margin calls. =E2=80=A6 The LME=
 had eventually intervened to halt trading a couple of hours after nickel h=
it $100,000. It also canceled billions of dollars of transactions, bringing=
 the price back to $48,078, where it closed the previous day, in what amoun=
ted to a lifeline for Xiang and Tsingshan.And then the LME said =E2=80=9Cwe=
ll, okay, $48,000?=E2=80=9D and Xiang again said =E2=80=9Cno thank you=E2=
=80=9D:To reopen the market, the LME proposed a solution: Xiang should stri=
ke a deal with holders of long positions to close out his trade. But a pric=
e of around $50,000 would be more than twice the level at which he had ente=
red his short position, and would mean accepting billions of dollars in los=
ses. ...Xiang told the assembled bankers he had no intention of closing the=
 position anywhere near $50,000. A few hours later he was delivering the sa=
me message to Matthew Chamberlain, chief executive of the LME. Tsingshan wa=
s a strong company, he said, and it=C2=A0had the support of the Chinese gov=
ernment. There would be no backing down.And so his banks said =E2=80=9Cwell=
 okay what price=C2=A0would=C2=A0be acceptable=E2=80=9D and he said =E2=80=
=9C$30,000=E2=80=9D and they said =E2=80=9Cfine=E2=80=9D:On March 14, a wee=
k after the chaos that engulfed the nickel market, Tsingshan announced a de=
al with its banks under which they agreed not to pursue the company for the=
 billions it owed for a period of time. In exchange, Xiang agreed a series =
of price levels at which he would reduce his nickel position once prices dr=
opped below about $30,000.=C2=A0Eventually nickel got below $30,000 and he =
got out of the position at about a $1 billion loss. =E2=80=9CThe loss has b=
een roughly offset by the profits of his nickel operations over the same pe=
riod.=E2=80=9DThe article also describes the scene at Xiang=E2=80=99s offic=
e on=C2=A0the evening of March 8:Within hours, more than 50 bankers had arr=
ived at his office wanting to hear how he planned to respond to the crisis.=
 He told them simply: =E2=80=9CI=E2=80=99m confident that we will overcome =
this.=E2=80=9DIf FIFTY BANKERS ever arrive at your office all at once, (1) =
you have done something terrible but (2) it is absolutely their problem, no=
t yours.With=C2=A0unprecedented chaos rippling=C2=A0through=C2=A0the indust=
ry,=C2=A0Xiang=C2=A0=E2=80=94 still facing his bankers in the early hours o=
f March 9 =E2=80=94=C2=A0had a key advantage. They were more terrified than=
 he was.If he refused to pay, they would have to chase him in courts in Ind=
onesia and China. What=E2=80=99s more, he had executed his nickel trade thr=
ough a variety of corporate entities =E2=80=93 such as the Hong Kong branch=
 of battery unit Ruipu Energy Co. =E2=80=93 and it wasn=E2=80=99t clear the=
 banks would even have the right to seize Tsingshan=E2=80=99s most valuable=
 assets.The bankers understood that if things went wrong, their careers wou=
ld be over, one person who was in the room remembered.Incredible stuff. Whe=
n the LME declared that the price of nickel wasn=E2=80=99t actually $100,00=
0 (as the market said) but $48,000, it broke a bunch of trades and cost som=
e financial traders hundreds of millions of dollars. They  were really mad,=
 understandably; several have  sued the  LME. I was pretty sympathetic to t=
hose lawsuits before reading this story, but now, oh man! The LME canceled =
trades and shut down the market not for some good neutral reason, but becau=
se it was bullied by a giant trader who decided that he preferred not to fo=
llow the rules, and the LME couldn=E2=80=99t risk the chaos of trying to ho=
ld him to the rules. So it put his losses on other people instead, people w=
ho did follow the rules and could meet their margin requirements.=C2=A0The =
point here I guess is that being responsible and well capitalized and corre=
ctly predicting the market price is fine, in financial markets, but it is d=
istinctly second-best. Being irresponsible and undercapitalized but giant i=
s much better. Then you can just=C2=A0tell=C2=A0the market what the price i=
s.

   =20
      GameStop stock split
   =20
 =20
We=E2=80=99re still  doing this huh:GameStop Corp. shares jumped 10% in the=
 opening minutes of Thursday=E2=80=99s session after announcing a four-for-=
one stock split in the form of a dividend, becoming one of the latest compa=
nies to do so as the practice has gained in popularity.Share splits had alm=
ost disappeared from U.S. stock markets before Apple Inc. and Tesla Inc. re=
vived the practice after splitting their stocks in 2020. Amazon.com Inc. fo=
llowed suit earlier this year. The moves helped trigger rallies in the comp=
anies=E2=80=99 shares as retail investors, who tend to favor stocks with lo=
wer price tags, flocked to them.=C2=A0GameStop has been beleaguered by ques=
tions about its business model and direction. At a time when consumers pref=
er to purchase video games digitally in online stores, GameStop has experim=
ented with pivots into esports and even crypto. The company became the post=
er child for so-called meme stocks, seeing volatile swings in the share pri=
ce over the last year that have had little to do with its business fundamen=
tals.=C2=A0Classically the reason to split a stock is to make it more affor=
dable for retail investors, though that reason has become less important as=
 (1) retail brokerage commissions went to zero and (2) retail brokerages st=
arted offering fractional-share trading. The postmodern meme-stock-y reason=
 to split a stock is to make=C2=A0YOLOing call options=C2=A0more affordable=
 for retail traders; options contracts still trade in units of 100 shares, =
and if you cut the share price by 75% then more people can afford to buy ca=
ll options, which is the preferred meme-stock way to push the stock up.=C2=
=A0I am not sure that GameStop can revive the magic of  last January, when =
retail traders bought stock and options and pushed its stock to the moon, b=
ut=C2=A0if you=E2=80=99re in the business of running a meme-stock company y=
ou do have to give your shareholders what they want and I suppose this is w=
hat they want. Seems fine. Here=E2=80=99s a guy:If GameStop split at its re=
cent after-hours levels, it would trade at $30.81.That is around the $30 pr=
e-split price target Wedbush analyst Michael Pachter assigns the stock, whi=
ch he rates at Underperform. =E2=80=9CMakes it more affordable for unsuspec=
ting rubes who haven=E2=80=99t yet lost all of their money,=E2=80=9D Pachte=
r told=C2=A0Barron=E2=80=99s=C2=A0via email when asked about the split.Sure=
ly they are=C2=A0suspecting=C2=A0rubes by now? Like at this point GameStop =
is its own special asset class; nobody is buying GameStop stock because the=
y walk by a GameStop store=C2=A0at the mall and are like =E2=80=9Coh GameSt=
op, I enjoy buying video games there, I should buy the stock, I=E2=80=99m s=
ure that an efficient market prices it appropriately so when I put in a mar=
ket order I will pay a fair price for the present value of its long-term ca=
sh flows.=E2=80=9D=C2=A0

   =20
      Vision
   =20
 =20
I  write a  lot around here about how good it can be, for your financial ca=
reer, to lose a bunch of money. Having your name attached to a huge success=
 is, of course, great. But having your name attached to a huge failure also=
 has its points. Being huge, in financial markets, is valuable in itself; i=
f you had a huge failure then that means that someone trusted you with a hu=
ge pot of money and you rewarded their trust by doing huge (though bad) thi=
ngs with it. You are both an effective salesperson and a risk-taker, which =
are strong points in your favor. Losing the money is bad, but presumably yo=
u learned something from the experience and won=E2=80=99t do that again.Als=
o, there are only so many big entities in finance, and if you lost a lot of=
 money at one of them then you might be well positioned to extract more=C2=
=A0money from that one. =E2=80=9CI know how to make a bunch of money off JP=
Morgan, because I lost a bunch of money for JPMorgan=E2=80=9D is a reasonab=
ly good pitch to a future employer who trades against JPMorgan.The SoftBank=
 Vision Fund is not exactly a huge failure =E2=80=94=C2=A0it seems to have =
made money, though it has underperformed public equities =E2=80=94 but it i=
s definitely=C2=A0huge ($100 billion), and it definitely=C2=A0had=C2=A0some=
 high-profile investment failures. And if you were involved with those fail=
ures then oh man have you learned some lessons! Who wouldn=E2=80=99t want t=
o take money from a venture capitalist who had an inside seat at the Vision=
 Fund? Like, for instance, Adam Neumann, the WeWork founder who is now doin=
g venture capital=C2=A0with some of the immense wealth that he extracted fr=
om SoftBank; here=E2=80=99s a Financial Times profile from March:His family=
 office, now a staff of more than 50 people in New York and Miami, has quie=
tly invested in 49 other start-ups, from a mortgage provider to a company a=
pplying artificial intelligence to=C2=A0in vitro fertilisation.This also pu=
ts Neumann himself in the position SoftBank=E2=80=99s Son occupied when he =
was still running WeWork. Back then, Son pushed Neumann to pursue ever-fast=
er growth. I ask Neumann how that experience informs how he operates now, a=
nd there is a five-second pause before he answers. =E2=80=9CThis is so inte=
resting. I=E2=80=99ve only shared this internally, so it almost feels weird=
 to say it out loud,=E2=80=9D he says, =E2=80=9Cbut I believe it=E2=80=99s =
that experience that is allowing me and the team now to be venture investor=
s=E2=80=89.=E2=80=89.=E2=80=89.=E2=80=89It=E2=80=99s actually taking, obvio=
usly, the successes. But even more interestingly, the lessons from the past=
 10 years, and implementing them to every situation that we see. And I woul=
dn=E2=80=99t be where I am today without that experience.=E2=80=9DIf you ar=
e a founder of a certain sort of fast-growing startup, some of the most imp=
ortant things for you to figure out might be:=09how to get SoftBank=E2=80=
=99s piles of money;=C2=A0=09whether to refuse SoftBank=E2=80=99s piles of =
money; and=09how not to ruin your company with SoftBank=E2=80=99s piles of =
money;and honestly Neumann might know more than anyone else alive about tho=
se topics. Might as well have him on your board.Anyway=C2=A0Rajeev Misra is=
 starting his own fund:Rajeev Misra is stepping back from his main roles at=
 SoftBank Group Corp., marking the exit of one of the key architects of the=
 Japanese conglomerate=E2=80=99s sometimes chaotic evolution into the world=
=E2=80=99s largest technology investor.=C2=A0A key lieutenant of SoftBank=
=E2=80=99s founder Masayoshi Son, Misra will retain a senior position with =
the group=E2=80=99s first $100 billion Vision Fund, but relinquish other ro=
les, people familiar with the matter said. =C2=A0Misra recently held talks =
with Son in Tokyo, during which he told his boss of his plans to leave to p=
ursue his own venture, according to the people, who asked not to be identif=
ied discussing confidential information.=C2=A0He=E2=80=99s already secured =
more than $6 billion in backing, including from Middle East investors, for =
a new fund that=E2=80=99ll target a mix of strategies, the people said.Ther=
e is going to be a whole club of SoftBank Cubs in venture capital. The sign=
al is not quite =E2=80=9Cthese people are good at identifying good private-=
company investment opportunities,=E2=80=9D but it is nonetheless a useful s=
ignal.

      The market can remain irrational longer etc.
   =20
 =20
Here=E2=80=99s a great trade:Korean crypto startup Uprise lost virtually al=
l of its client funds by shorting luna (LUNA) during its price crash and ge=
tting caught on the bounces, Seoul Economic daily reported on Wednesday. ..=
.Uprise=E2=80=99s AI-enabled trading technology was supposed to minimize th=
e risk associated with leveraged crypto trading.However, the system could n=
ot prevent the firm from being liquidated out of its LUNA futures trading p=
osition and losing 26.7 billion won ($20 million) in the process. This happ=
ened during Luna's price crash. It was reportedly shorting Luna =E2=80=94 w=
hile its price plummeted =E2=80=94 but got caught out during sudden price p=
umps along the way.I feel like when I was a youngster the warning you got a=
bout short selling was =E2=80=9Cif you short a stock, you have unlimited ri=
sk, because=C2=A0the stock can go to infinity,=C2=A0but limited upside,=C2=
=A0because the stock can=E2=80=99t go below zero.=E2=80=9D The classic reto=
rt is =E2=80=9CI=E2=80=99ve seen a lot of stocks go to zero, but I=E2=80=99=
ve never seen one go to infinity.=E2=80=9DA more fundamental problem is tha=
t short selling is sort of necessarily=C2=A0leveraged. If you buy a stock f=
or $100 with your own money, and it falls to $0.01, you can hold on to the =
stock, no problem: You=E2=80=99ll never get a margin call, because you neve=
r borrowed money; you=E2=80=99ll never need to put up more than the origina=
l $100. And then if it recovers to $200 you make money.=C2=A0But if you sho=
rt a stock at $100, you have to post collateral with your stock lender,=C2=
=A0and if the stock goes to $500 then you have to post more collateral, and=
 if you can=E2=80=99t you get liquidated. So if you=E2=80=99re like =E2=80=
=9CI=E2=80=99ve got $20 million to bet that Luna is going to zero,=E2=80=9D=
 you=E2=80=99re right, but it doesn=E2=80=99t help.
 =20
   =20
      Use your stablecoins to back CDS on private-credit securitizations
   =20
 =20
I don=E2=80=99t know  whatever man:Credit default swaps are hard enough to =
figure out.=C2=A0Shadow lenders=C2=A0and their repackaging of cash flows fr=
om loans into securities come with their own complexities. And cryptocurren=
cies, well, they can be as mind-bending as anything financial alchemists ha=
ve ever dreamed up.A new product combines all three into one.It=E2=80=99s f=
rom fintech company Percent Technologies and Anzen, a new player in the cor=
ner of crypto known as decentralized finance, or DeFi. The idea is to use c=
apital that crypto enthusiasts have stashed into stablecoins to offer inves=
tors in Percent=E2=80=99s high-yield securitizations protection from a defa=
ult. =E2=80=A6Adding to the uncertainty, Anzen has only been around since J=
anuary, its plans to attract outside capital and generate returns are vague=
, and its founders are anonymous.That hasn=E2=80=99t deterred Percent found=
er and Chief Executive=C2=A0Nelson Chu=C2=A0from forging ahead with the par=
tnership, which will allow buyers of Percent=E2=80=99s structured notes to =
receive a payout if defaults in the underlying loans rise above a pre-defin=
ed threshold, similarly to what happens in the multi-trillion-dollar market=
 for credit default swaps. =E2=80=A6In a crucial difference with the tradit=
ional CDS market, capital to cover potential losses in the Percent investme=
nts will not come from an institution taking the opposite side of the trade=
.=C2=A0Instead -- and this is where the DeFi magic comes in -- it will come=
 from a reserve fund consisting of stablecoins=C2=A0staked=C2=A0on the Anze=
n protocol as well as interest and principal amortization payments contribu=
ted by Percent.=C2=A0Like, Percent structures private-credit loans, package=
s them into notes and sells that risk to investors? Then it buys back (some=
 of) the credit risk and sells it again to the DeFi people in the Anzen pro=
tocol? I guess the promise of decentralized finance really is =E2=80=9Canyo=
ne can invest in synthetic securitizations of private-credit risk=E2=80=9D =
but, you know, why?
 =20
   =20
      Should index funds be illegal?
   =20
 =20
We  talked the other day about a paper finding =E2=80=9Cthat establishments=
 of firms that experience an increase in ownership by larger and more conce=
ntrated institutional shareholders have lower employment and wages.=E2=80=
=9D I took this as evidence for the idea that the rise of institutional com=
mon ownership gives shareholders more power to negotiate against labor: =E2=
=80=9CIf you are a universal owner of all companies,=E2=80=9D I said, =E2=
=80=9Cwhat you want is just for wages to be lower everywhere.=E2=80=9DBut a=
ctually it is more nuanced than that; from the paper:We find that only inst=
itutional=C2=A0shareholders with control motives =E2=80=93 i.e., =E2=80=9Cd=
edicated=E2=80=9D institutions based on the classification of Bushee (1998)=
 or =E2=80=9Cactivist=E2=80=9D institutions =E2=80=93 have a strong negativ=
e association with employment and payroll. Other types of shareholders with=
out control motives, such as =E2=80=9Cquasi-index=E2=80=9D and =E2=80=9Ctra=
nsient=E2=80=9D institutions, have a weaker and if anything, positive relat=
ion with employment and payroll. Anecdotal evidence from shareholder activi=
sm campaigns further points to the maximization of shareholder value as the=
 stated goal to reduce labor-related expenses.It=E2=80=99s not that common =
owners reduce employment; it=E2=80=99s that activist institutional investor=
s=C2=A0do. There is some evidence that index funds make activism easier =E2=
=80=94 that activists are the ones doing the work to represent the sharehol=
der class, sometimes with the  quiet encouragement of the big universal own=
ers =E2=80=94=C2=A0but the index funds themselves are not pushing wages dow=
n.

5px;">Nickel guy</h2>
</td>

</table><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">If you sell nickel futures at a price =
of $25,000 per ton, and then the price of nickel futures goes up to $100,00=
0 per ton, then in some simple arithmetic sense you have lost $75,000 per t=
on. If you sold 100 tons of nickel futures, then you have lost more than $7=
=C2=A0million. But if you sold <em>150,000 tons</em>=C2=A0of futures, the m=
ath changes a bit; it becomes non-linear and relativistic. If you sold 150,=
000 tons of nickel futures at $25,000 per ton, and then the price goes up t=
o $100,000, your banks will call you up and say =E2=80=9Cuh you have lost $=
11 billion, can you pay that please,=E2=80=9D and you will say =E2=80=9CI w=
ould prefer not to,=E2=80=9D and an insane series of events will happen:</p=
><ol style=3D"list-style-type: decimal; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;=
">=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">The nickel exchange will <a href=3D"=
https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/28298571.260573/aHR0cHM6Ly93d=
3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjItMDMtMDgvbmlja2VsLWlzLWNh=

none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; bo=
rder-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">  cancel a bunch of trad=
es</a> and declare that=C2=A0<em>actually=C2=A0</em>the market price of nic=
kel is $48,000 per ton, magically reversing most=C2=A0of your losses.</li>=
=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Then the exchange will call you and sa=
y =E2=80=9Cokay let=E2=80=99s close you out of that trade at $48,000 per to=
n.=E2=80=9D</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Then you will say =E2=
=80=9Cno, this is still too much money for me to lose, I prefer not to.=E2=
=80=9D</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Then your banks will say =
=E2=80=9Cwell okay how much are you willing to lose?=E2=80=9D</li>=09<li st=
yle=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">You will say =E2=80=9CI would close out this tr=
ade at $30,000, that=E2=80=99s how much money I am willing to lose.=E2=80=
=9D</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Your banks will say =E2=80=9Co=
kay fine, we=E2=80=99ll wait for nickel prices to go back below $30,000, me=
anwhile we=E2=80=99ll just lend you the money to stay in the position.=E2=
=80=9D</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">They will.</li>=09<li style=
=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Eventually nickel prices will go below $30,000 and=
 you will get out of the trade at a modest loss.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin=
-bottom: 5px;">If prices never go below $30,000 then I guess your banks are=
 very sad, but honestly they=E2=80=99re pretty sad about all of this anyway=
.</li></ol><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I cannot stress enough that this is=
 not how it works if you are a small customer. This is the white-glove trea=
tment that only the biggest customers get. If you are big enough, you get t=
o=C2=A0<em>tell the exchange how much money you=E2=80=99re willing to lose<=
/em>, and the exchange and your banks will make sure you don=E2=80=99t lose=
 more than that.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Here is a wild <a href=3D"=

<blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">After nickel started spiking on Ma=

ang again said =E2=80=9Cno thank you=E2=80=9D:</p><blockquote><p style=3D"m=
argin: 16px 0;">To reopen the market, the LME proposed a solution: Xiang sh=
ould strike a deal with holders of long positions to close out his trade. B=
ut a price of around $50,000 would be more than twice the level at which he=
 had entered his short position, and would mean accepting billions of dolla=
rs in losses. ...</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Xiang told the assembled =
bankers he had no intention of closing the position anywhere near $50,000. =
A few hours later he was delivering the same message to Matthew Chamberlain=
, chief executive of the LME. Tsingshan was a strong company, he said, and =
it=C2=A0had the support of the Chinese government. There would be no backin=
g down.</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">And so his banks said =

=9D:</p><blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">On March 14, a week after =
the chaos that engulfed the nickel market, Tsingshan announced a deal with =
its banks under which they agreed not to pursue the company for the billion=
s it owed for a period of time. In exchange, Xiang agreed a series of price=
 levels at which he would reduce his nickel position once prices dropped be=
low about $30,000.=C2=A0</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Event=

 billion loss. =E2=80=9CThe loss has been roughly offset by the profits of =
his nickel operations over the same period.=E2=80=9D</p><p style=3D"margin:=
 16px 0;">The article also describes the scene at Xiang=E2=80=99s office on=

ithin hours, more than 50 bankers had arrived at his office wanting to hear=

=E2=80=99m confident that we will overcome this.=E2=80=9D</p></blockquote><=
p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">If FIFTY BANKERS ever arrive at your office all=
 at once, (1) you have done something terrible but (2) it is absolutely the=
ir problem, not yours.</p><blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">With=C2=
=A0unprecedented chaos rippling=C2=A0through=C2=A0the industry,=C2=A0Xiang=

=80=94=C2=A0had a key advantage. They were more terrified than he was.</p><=
p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">If he refused to pay, they would have to chase =
him in courts in Indonesia and China. What=E2=80=99s more, he had executed =

=E2=80=99t clear the banks would even have the right to seize Tsingshan=E2=
=80=99s most valuable assets.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The bankers u=

n who was in the room remembered.</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px =

blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I feel like when I was a youngster =
the warning you got about short selling was =E2=80=9Cif you short a stock, =
you have unlimited risk, because=C2=A0the stock can go to infinity,=C2=A0bu=
t limited upside,=C2=A0because the stock can=E2=80=99t go below zero.=E2=80=
=9D The classic retort is =E2=80=9CI=E2=80=99ve seen a lot of stocks go to =
zero, but I=E2=80=99ve never seen one go to infinity.=E2=80=9D</p><p style=
=3D"margin: 16px 0;">A more fundamental problem is that short selling is so=
rt of necessarily=C2=A0<em>leveraged</em>. If you buy a stock for $100 with=
 your own money, and it falls to $0.01, you can hold on to the stock, no pr=
oblem: You=E2=80=99ll never get a margin call, because you never borrowed m=
oney; you=E2=80=99ll never need to put up more than the original $100. And =
then if it recovers to $200 you make money.=C2=A0But if you short a stock a=
t $100, you have to post collateral with your stock lender,=C2=A0and if the=
 stock goes to $500 then you have to post more collateral, and if you can=
=E2=80=99t you get liquidated. So if you=E2=80=99re like =E2=80=9CI=E2=80=
=99ve got $20 million to bet that Luna is going to zero,=E2=80=9D you=E2=80=
=99re right, but it doesn=E2=80=99t help.</p><table class=3D"header" width=

n: 16px 0;">Credit default swaps are hard enough to figure out.=C2=A0Shadow=

s come with their own complexities. And cryptocurrencies, well, they can be=
 as mind-bending as anything financial alchemists have ever dreamed up.</p>=
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">A new product combines all three into one.</p>=
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">It=E2=80=99s from fintech company Percent Tech=
nologies and Anzen, a new player in the corner of crypto known as decentral=
ized finance, or DeFi. The idea is to use capital that crypto enthusiasts h=
ave stashed into stablecoins to offer investors in Percent=E2=80=99s high-y=
ield securitizations protection from a default. =E2=80=A6</p><p style=3D"ma=
rgin: 16px 0;">Adding to the uncertainty, Anzen has only been around since =
January, its plans to attract outside capital and generate returns are vagu=
e, and its founders are anonymous.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">That has=
n=E2=80=99t deterred Percent founder and Chief Executive=C2=A0Nelson Chu=C2=
=A0from forging ahead with the partnership, which will allow buyers of Perc=
ent=E2=80=99s structured notes to receive a payout if defaults in the under=
lying loans rise above a pre-defined threshold, similarly to what happens i=
n the multi-trillion-dollar market for credit default swaps. =E2=80=A6</p><=
p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">In a crucial difference with the traditional CD=
S market, capital to cover potential losses in the Percent investments will=
 not come from an institution taking the opposite side of the trade.=C2=A0<=
/p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Instead -- and this is where the DeFi magic=
 comes in -- it will come from a reserve fund consisting of stablecoins=C2=
=A0staked=C2=A0on the Anzen protocol as well as interest and principal amor=
tization payments contributed by Percent.=C2=A0</p></blockquote><p style=3D=
"margin: 16px 0;">Like, Percent structures private-credit loans, packages t=
hem into notes and sells that risk to investors? Then it buys back (some of=
) the credit risk and sells it again to the DeFi people in the Anzen protoc=
ol? I guess the promise of decentralized finance really is =E2=80=9Canyone =
can invest in synthetic securitizations of private-credit risk=E2=80=9D but=
